cited_text: When the unknown is a better option than the known, run in that direction, Run towards darkness. And the unknown was what I didn't know, right, the advertising business. But the known was this industry that was going in the wrong direction, in a direction where they were selling one song for a lot of money.
response_text: The framing here is useful — it's not reckless risk-taking, it's a **directional bet** based on reading which side of a fork is decaying. Stoute knew the music industry's CD monopoly was structurally broken, so the unknown was the lower-risk option by comparison.

cited_text: Black consumers are the best consumers in the world because they buy products that aren't marketed to them. I grew up in a world where I'd see African American women buying products. L' Oreal products in which There was only white women with blonde hair on the box or in a commercial, and African American women would buy it, or the same thing with African American men. And people took advantage of that. But when brands leaned into being authentically connected to where culture was, which also spoke to those segments, they were taking market share, they were taking that attention.
response_text: This is a sharp observation about **market inefficiency**: brands were extracting value from Black consumers while giving them nothing in return culturally. Stoute's insight was that closing that gap wasn't charity — it was an arbitrage opportunity.

cited_text: When I left the record business, I was making a couple million dollars a year at 29 and I went into an agency in which I got paid $150,000 and 20% equity. I didn't necessarily understand the value of equity at all. I certainly understood 150,000 was a lot less than 2 million, two and a half million. But I wasn't even betting on the equity man. I was really betting on the education. I sincerely mean that. I did not know what was going to happen at all, but I knew I was going to learn.
response_text: Dropping from $2M to $150K and framing it as **tuition** rather than a pay cut is a clean mental model. Most people can't make that trade because they conflate income with identity.

cited_text: I'm like, I don't even know what you're saying. It's not a TV commercial. It's entertaining content, whether it's a music video, right? Which was Men in black or a 60 second same idea. But you, you call it a TV commercial only because you run it on television, not because the format is different, just because you're running it on television. So music videos ran on mtv, but if you took the music video off MTV and made it a 60 second spot, just run it on television, it's a television commercial.
response_text: The **format obsession** of the ad industry was always a distraction from the underlying question: does it hold attention? Stoute collapsed the distinction between music video and ad 20 years before everyone else had to.

cited_text: We come back in the room, this 18 year old kid says, no, I'm betting on myself. He didn't say that, but we knew that he was going to take the other meetings. He walked away from $10 million check sign to his name to go back to the projects in Akron and back to homeroom. I applauded him because I knew at that moment in time that the world had changed. When a young African American that was in poverty knew that his talent was bigger than that check. It taught me a lot. It taught me a lot of where the world was going and that we were already there.
response_text: The LeBron story works as a **proof point for self-belief** at scale — not just personal conviction but a signal about where an entire generation was heading. Stoute reads it correctly as a cultural leading indicator, not just an anecdote.

response_text: The record labels deliberately left fan data on the table — not through incompetence, but to keep artists dependent.

cited_text: If the artists knew who their fans were, they wouldn't need a record company at all. response_text: 200,000 true fans — with direct contact and CRM — is enough to build a serious business through music plus high-margin merchandise.

cited_text: Prince changed his name from Prince to a symbol to get out of a contract because Warner owned his name, image and Likeness. response_text: He wrote "Slave" on his face — one of the most successful artists ever — because he didn't own the IP that came out of his own brain.

cited_text: I said, could you imagine if every time a seed investor came in, they actually own the IP and an intellectual property? They owned it. That's what it is in the music business. response_text: That's exactly what happens in music — the first check-writer owns your name and likeness.

cited_text: So what he did when he negotiated his deal with Warner to put out Sinners, which he wrote and directed, was that he would only put it with a studio that would allow him to get the rights back in a certain period of time. response_text: Coogler's Sinners deal — rights revert to him after a period — is unprecedented at studio scale, with hundreds of millions on the line.

cited_text: That was really what I was the idea like, in my heart of hearts, building a company today, if you want to disrupt and do something that is groundbreaking, you have to live at the convergence of culture, technology and storytelling. response_text: The thesis of UnitedMasters in one sentence.

cited_text: Bono said something to me one time which is so profound that we should all take with us, which is you can get anything done in this world if you're willing to not take credit. If you can actually remove that feeling of needing to be self applauded for doing that, you can get anything done.

response_text: Stoute watched Kobe's locker and his practice routine and saw the same person.

cited_text: Nas and Jay Z are in that conversation. They're that good. The way they can paint pictures that you would never. response_text: Stoute's point is that the medium obscures the craft — the beats, the clothing, the media create noise around what are genuinely great writers.
